title: Tar on Carpet
timeEstimate: 30 minutes to several hours; flammable solvent safety mandatory
stainChemistry: Tar (bitumen / asphalt) is a complex petroleum-derived mixture: polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, resins, and heavy petroleum fractions. It bonds to carpet through strong hydrophobic + mechanical adhesion. Fresh tar is sticky and semi-fluid; as volatile fractions evaporate it hardens into a tough, black, rubbery deposit that resists water and most common cleaners. The only thing that dissolves tar reliably is petroleum-based solvent — mineral spirits, POG, kerosene-type products ("like dissolves like"). Home equivalents: Goo Gone, Tarx, De-Solv-It. All are FLAMMABLE — the same property that makes them dissolve tar makes them a fire hazard. Ventilation + no-flame + no-pouring is the safety discipline that can't be skipped.
whyThisWorks: Mechanical removal of bulk softened tar (with cold harden + scrape, or solvent softening + lift) before solvent dissolution reduces the amount of solvent needed. Solvent on a cloth dissolves residual tar and transfers it to the cloth. Dawn emulsifies the solvent residue. Patience + multiple passes > more aggressive solvent + fewer passes. Pouring solvent into carpet = pad saturation = permanent + fire hazard.
defaultAssumption: Summer context — fresh road tar tracked in on shoes, shingle tar from roof work, driveway sealer walked into the house. Fresh tar is sticky and harder to chunk off; partially-cool tar can be freeze-scraped. Old dried tar is black, rubbery, and takes multiple solvent passes. Large spills (someone dropped a tar bucket) = pro — home can't safely manage the solvent volume.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Open windows, fan out, gloves, mask if sensitive
spottingProtocol.instruction: Before any solvent: open windows, turn on exhaust fan, turn OFF gas stove pilot lights if near (gas range / water heater), no candles, no smoking, no one frying anything in the kitchen. Wear gloves. Petroleum solvent fumes are heavier than air and can accumulate in low spaces. If the spill is large (more than a palm), you may not be able to ventilate adequately at home — pro.

spottingProtocol.agent: Ice-Bag Freeze Brittle (for Fresh / Pliable Tar)
spottingProtocol.equipment: Ice in sealed plastic bag, plastic card
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place ice sealed in a bag on fresh/pliable tar. 10–15 min hardens it brittle. Crack and lift bulk pieces off with a plastic card. Never direct ice. For already-hard / aged tar, skip this — go to solvent.

spottingProtocol.agent: Goo Gone / Mineral Spirits — Tamp From Top
spottingProtocol.equipment: Goo Gone or mineral spirits on white cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply solvent to a white cloth (never pour on carpet). Press and tamp the remaining tar from the top. Flip cloth as it picks up tar. Work outside-in toward the center. 3–5 passes typical for fresh tar; 6–10 for aged. Ventilate the whole time. No flames.

spottingProtocol.agent: Dawn Damp Cloth — Emulsify Solvent Residue
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn + cool water on damp cloth, soft brush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dawn in cool water on damp cloth. Work with soft toothbrush. Clears solvent + last tar traces. Damp-cloth rinse — never pour.

spottingProtocol.agent: Weighted Dry Towel + Fan — Vent Solvent Vapor
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dry towel + weight, fan, open windows
spottingProtocol.instruction: Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan the area several hours with windows open to vent remaining solvent vapor. Don't walk on the spot until fully dry. Don't close the room up while solvent is still evaporating.

spottingProtocol.agent: Inspect + Repeat Solvent Tamp If Needed
spottingProtocol.instruction: Inspect in daylight. Residual black = more solvent passes (patience + multiple passes beats aggressive solvent). Ventilate between rounds.

steps: 1. Ventilate room, no flame, no smoking, gloves.
2. Ice-bag freeze for fresh/pliable tar. Crack and lift bulk.
3. Mineral spirits / POG / Goo Gone tamped from top, outside-in.
4. Dawn / NSD damp cloth + soft brush.
5. Weighted dry towel + fan + continued ventilation.
6. Repeat solvent tamp for residual — multiple passes over aggression.
warnings: FLAMMABLE SOLVENT — ventilate, no flame, no smoking, no running gas pilots nearby
NEVER pour solvent into carpet — pad saturation + fire hazard
Solvent vapor is heavier than air — ventilate low spaces
Large spill (>palm sized) = pro — home ventilation insufficient
Olefin carpet: test solvent at hidden seam first

homeSolutions.agent: Safety First — This Is a Flammable Solvent Job
homeSolutions.instruction: Tar only dissolves in petroleum-based solvents — Goo Gone, mineral spirits, similar. Those solvents are FLAMMABLE, and their vapor is heavier than air and settles into low spaces (basement carpet, ground-floor rooms). Before you start: open windows wide, turn on an exhaust fan, blow air out through the window. NO flames in the house — that means no candles, no gas stove lit, no cigarettes, no pilot lights on a gas water heater in an attached closet. Wear gloves. If the tar spot is larger than your palm, you may not be able to ventilate a home well enough — call a pro.

If the tar is still soft or sticky (fresh road tar, tracked-in shingle tar), press a zip-lock bag of ice directly on it for 10–15 minutes. Tar hardens brittle. Use the edge of a plastic card or dull knife to crack and lift the bulk pieces off. Never put ice directly on the carpet — water damage. For already-hard / aged tar, skip this and go to the solvent step.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone on a White Rag — Never Poured
homeSolutions.instruction: Put Goo Gone (orange bottle, hardware / grocery store) on a clean white cotton rag. DO NOT pour Goo Gone on the carpet — pouring sinks solvent and tar into the pad where you can't get them out, and creates a fire hazard. Press and tamp the rag on the remaining tar from the TOP. Flip the rag as it picks up black. Work from the OUTSIDE of the spot toward the CENTER — the counter-intuitive direction — so you don't spread the edge. Expect 3–5 passes for fresh tar, 6–10 for aged. Patience + multiple passes beats aggressive application.

homeSolutions.agent: If Goo Gone Plateaus — Mineral Spirits
homeSolutions.instruction: If Goo Gone stops progressing on stubborn tar, mineral spirits (paint thinner, any hardware store) is stronger. Same technique — cloth, tamp, never pour, ventilate the whole time. Mineral spirits is MORE flammable than Goo Gone; be extra careful about flames and ventilation. Don't use both at once; work one through completely before switching.
Work a drop of Dawn in cool water into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Emulsifies the solvent residue and lifts any last tar. Damp-cloth rinse — don't pour water.

homeSolutions.agent: Ventilated Fan-Dry — Don't Close the Room
homeSolutions.instruction: Stack dry paper towels on the spot, heavy book on top, 30 minutes. Point a fan at the area for SEVERAL HOURS with windows still open. Solvent vapor keeps evaporating for a while — if you close the room up, it accumulates. Don't walk on the spot until fully dry. Don't light anything near the area until you can't smell solvent.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Large Spills, Wool, or Olefin
homeSolutions.instruction: Pro if: the spill is larger than a palm, the carpet is wool (pro-only, pH / solvent sensitivity), the carpet is Berber / indoor-outdoor (often olefin, solvent-sensitive — test at hidden seam first), or the tar survives multiple solvent rounds. Tell them: "Tar on [nylon / polyester / olefin / wool] carpet, source [road / shingle / sealer], age [fresh / aged], home-treated with Goo Gone [+ mineral spirits] tamped from top + Dawn. No pouring. No flames. [Residual black / fiber concern]."

steps: 1. SAFETY — ventilate, no flames, no smoking, no gas pilots nearby. Gloves.
3. Goo Gone on RAG, tamp from top, outside-in. NEVER pour.
4. If stalled: mineral spirits same technique, more caution.
6. Weighted towel + fan-dry SEVERAL hours with windows open.
7. Large spill / wool / olefin / residual = pro.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the spill is larger than a palm (ventilation insufficient at home), if the carpet is wool or Berber/olefin with solvent sensitivity, if tar survives 10+ solvent passes, or if you can't safely ventilate and kill all flames in the home.
warnings: FLAMMABLE SOLVENT — ventilate, no flames, no smoking, no pilots
NEVER pour solvent on carpet — pad soak + fire hazard
Solvent vapor is heavier than air — settles in low spaces
Never rub — drags tar through fiber
Olefin / Berber = hidden-seam solvent test first

neverDo: Never use solvent near open flame, pilot light, or hot equipment
Never pour solvent — tamp with cloth
Never light anything in the room until solvent smell is fully gone
Never smoke during or after solvent use
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester carpet: tolerates Goo Gone + mineral spirits
Olefin (polypropylene): test solvent at hidden seam — some soften
Wool: pro only — solvent + pH risk

materialWarnings: Vapor settles low — ventilate low spaces
Patience + multiple passes beats aggressive solvent
Never pour — always tamp with cloth

escalation.when: Spill larger than a palm, carpet is wool / Berber / olefin with solvent concerns, multiple solvent rounds plateau, or you can't adequately ventilate / kill flames at home.
escalation.whatToTell: "Tar on [nylon / polyester / olefin / wool] carpet, source [road / shingle / sealer], age [fresh / aged], home-treated with Goo Gone [+ mineral spirits] tamped from top + Dawn. No pouring. No flames. [Residual black / fiber concerns / large spill]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with solvent spotting + adequate extraction ventilation

contextQuestions: Source — road tar, roofing shingle, driveway sealer?
Carpet fiber — nylon / polyester / olefin / wool?
Can you fully ventilate the room and kill all flames?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh road tar is the easiest variant — ice freeze + lift gets most of it off before solvent is needed. Driveway sealer is similar chemistry but often aged by the time it's noticed. Shingle tar from roof work often has embedded grit that damages fiber when scraped. Large spills are the honest pro-escalation — home ventilation for solvent scales poorly past a palm-sized area.
